body: Proper brushing technique is essential for removing plaque and preventing gum disease. Use a soft-bristled toothbrush and hold it at a 45-degree angle to the gums. Use gentle circular motions, brushing all surfaces — outer, inner, and chewing surfaces. Brush for at least two minutes twice daily. Avoid scrubbing side-to-side, which can cause enamel wear and gum recession. Replace your toothbrush every three to four months or sooner if bristles are frayed.
title: Proper Brushing Technique — Patient Education Guide

body: Flossing removes plaque and food particles from between teeth and under the gumline where a toothbrush cannot reach. Use approximately 18 inches of floss, winding most around the middle fingers of each hand. Guide the floss between teeth with a gentle zigzag motion. Curve the floss around each tooth and slide it gently under the gumline. Use a clean section of floss for each tooth. Floss at least once per day, preferably before bedtime. Patients with bridges or implants may benefit from floss threaders or interdental brushes.
title: Flossing Technique and Interdental Cleaning — Reference

body: Diet plays a significant role in oral health. Fermentable carbohydrates such as sugars and refined starches feed oral bacteria, which produce acids that erode enamel. Frequency of sugar exposure is more damaging than total amount — sipping sweetened drinks throughout the day is more harmful than drinking them with a meal. Calcium-rich foods like dairy, leafy greens, and almonds help strengthen tooth enamel. Crunchy fruits and vegetables stimulate saliva flow, which neutralizes acids. Patients should limit acidic beverages and rinse with water after consuming them.
title: Diet and Oral Health — Patient Education Guide

body: Periodontal charting is a systematic clinical assessment of the attachment levels and pocket depths around each tooth. Use a calibrated periodontal probe to measure six sites per tooth: mesiobuccal, buccal, distobuccal, mesiolingual, lingual, and distolingual. Record probing depths in millimeters. Note bleeding on probing (BOP), furcation involvement, recession, and mobility. Chart findings in the patient's electronic health record. Charting should be performed at every comprehensive exam and at appropriate recall intervals based on patient risk. Compare to previous charting to monitor changes in attachment levels.
title: Periodontal Charting Procedure — Staff Guide

body: All reusable dental instruments must be sterilized between patient appointments following OSHA and CDC guidelines. After use, instruments are rinsed and placed in a puncture-resistant container. Transport to the sterilization area and clean in an ultrasonic cleaner for the manufacturer-recommended cycle time. Rinse thoroughly and inspect for residual debris. Package instruments in sterilization pouches and label with a date and cycle indicator. Process in an autoclave at 132°C for a minimum of four minutes (flash) or 121°C for 15 minutes (gravity). Verify cycle success using chemical and biological indicators. Store sterile packages in a clean, dry area and treat as contaminated if the package integrity is compromised.
title: Instrument Sterilization Protocol — Reference

body: Fluoride plays a critical role in preventing dental caries by remineralizing enamel and inhibiting acid production by oral bacteria. Professional fluoride treatments are recommended for patients at moderate to high caries risk. Apply fluoride varnish (5% sodium fluoride) or gel (1.23% APF) following a prophylaxis. Fluoride varnish is preferred for most patients due to its safety profile, especially in children. Advise patients to avoid eating or drinking for 30 minutes following varnish application. Document the type and concentration of fluoride applied in the patient chart. Reassess fluoride therapy needs at each recall appointment.
title: Fluoride Application Protocol — Staff Guide

body: This CE course summary covers advances in ultrasonic scaling and root planing techniques for the treatment of periodontal disease. Topics include: selection of ultrasonic insert types (magnetostrictive vs. piezoelectric), power and water settings for different clinical situations, adaptation of the insert tip to tooth anatomy, subgingival debridement strategies, and patient comfort management. Learning objectives: differentiate clinical indications for hand vs. ultrasonic instrumentation, demonstrate proper insert adaptation, and apply evidence-based protocols for non-surgical periodontal therapy. Approved for 2 CE credit hours by the Academy of General Dentistry.
title: CE Course Summary: Advances in Ultrasonic Scaling (2 CE Credits)

body: Dental anxiety is one of the most common barriers to regular dental care. Hygienists play a key role in identifying and managing anxious patients. Strategies include: establishing trust through clear communication, explaining procedures before performing them, using the 'tell-show-do' technique, offering the patient signal words to pause the appointment, and adjusting the pace of treatment. For patients with severe anxiety, coordinate with the dentist regarding sedation options. Document anxiety level in the patient chart and note successful management strategies for future appointments. Follow-up calls after appointments can improve patient retention.
title: Managing Dental Anxiety — Patient Communication Guide
